seo.title: E-Commerce RAG Pipeline AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for rag pipeline AI agents in e-commerce. Covers FTC advertising guidelines, CCPA/GDPR, PCI DSS for payments.

content.intro: Deploying a RAG pipeline in e-commerce introduces compounding risks: retrieval errors can surface outdated pricing, fabricated product claims, or misrepresented return policies at scale, triggering FTC enforcement and customer disputes. Unlike static content, LLM synthesis can hallucinate details not present in retrieved documents, making source faithfulness verification a non-negotiable safeguard. This checklist operationalizes compliance controls across the full RAG stack—from document ingestion to final response delivery—so your AI agent earns customer trust rather than eroding it.

sections.heading: Data Ingestion & Knowledge Base Integrity
sections.description: The quality and freshness of your vector store directly determines whether the RAG pipeline surfaces accurate product, pricing, and policy information. Stale or malformed source documents are the root cause of most downstream compliance failures.

items.label: Implement real-time price sync from authoritative PIM/ERP
items.description: Configure automated ingestion pipelines that pull pricing data directly from your Product Information Management or ERP system on every price change event, not on a fixed schedule. Stale prices in the vector store are the leading cause of pricing misrepresentation violations under FTC guidelines.

items.label: Tag every document chunk with a TTL (time-to-live) expiry
items.description: Assign expiration metadata to each indexed chunk—product descriptions (7 days), pricing (24 hours), return policies (30 days)—and build an automated purge job that removes expired chunks before they can be retrieved and synthesized.

items.label: Validate product claim substantiation before indexing
items.description: Before any product description or marketing copy is ingested into the vector store, run it through a claim-substantiation check that flags unverified superlatives (e.g., 'best,' 'clinically proven') and requires a linked evidence document per FTC advertising guidelines.

items.label: Maintain a versioned audit log of all document ingestion events
items.description: Log every document add, update, and delete operation with timestamps, source system identifiers, and the user or service account that triggered the change. This log is essential for reconstructing what information was available to the RAG pipeline at the time of any disputed customer interaction.

items.label: Enforce schema validation on return and refund policy documents
items.description: Define a structured schema for return/refund policy documents (eligibility window, restocking fees, exclusions, process steps) and reject any ingestion that fails schema validation. Unstructured or partial policy documents cause the LLM to synthesize incomplete or misleading policy summaries.

items.label: Segregate PCI-scoped data from the RAG knowledge base
items.description: Ensure that no cardholder data, payment credentials, or PCI DSS-scoped transaction records are ever ingested into the vector store. Implement an allowlist of permitted document types and a blocklist scanner that rejects any chunk containing card number patterns, CVV fields, or bank account data.

items.label: Deduplicate conflicting product records before indexing
items.description: Run a deduplication pass that identifies multiple versions of the same product (by SKU or GTIN) and resolves conflicts by selecting the most recently updated authoritative record. Conflicting chunks cause the LLM to synthesize contradictory product specifications.

sections.heading: Retrieval Quality & Hallucination Prevention
sections.description: The retrieval and context assembly layer determines what evidence the LLM is given to synthesize a response. Weak retrieval—wrong chunks, low-relevance results, or missing context—is the primary driver of hallucinated product claims and policy misrepresentations.

items.label: Set a minimum cosine similarity threshold for retrieved chunks
items.description: Configure your vector search to return results only above a calibrated similarity threshold (e.g., 0.78). Below this threshold, the system should return a fallback response directing the customer to a human agent rather than allowing the LLM to synthesize from weakly relevant context.

items.label: Implement a cross-encoder reranker to validate context relevance
items.description: After initial vector retrieval, pass the top-k chunks through a cross-encoder reranker to score true semantic relevance to the query. Discard chunks that fall below the reranker threshold before passing context to the LLM, reducing the risk of off-topic content being synthesized into the response.

items.label: Cap context window to prevent conflicting information injection
items.description: Limit the number of retrieved chunks passed to the LLM to a tested maximum (e.g., 5 chunks) and implement a conflict detection step that flags when retrieved chunks contain contradictory pricing or policy statements, triggering a human review escalation instead of LLM synthesis.

items.label: Deploy a post-generation faithfulness scorer on every response
items.description: After the LLM generates a response, run it through an NLI-based faithfulness scorer (e.g., using a fine-tuned entailment model) that verifies every factual claim in the output is entailed by the retrieved source chunks. Responses with faithfulness scores below threshold must be blocked or flagged before delivery.

items.label: Implement query intent classification to route high-risk queries
items.description: Classify incoming queries by intent (pricing inquiry, return policy, product specification, complaint) and route high-risk intents—such as price matching requests or refund eligibility questions—through a stricter retrieval pipeline with mandatory human-in-the-loop review before response delivery.

items.label: Log all retrieval results alongside final responses for auditability
items.description: For every customer interaction, persist the exact chunks retrieved, their similarity scores, the assembled context, and the final LLM response in a linked audit record. This enables post-hoc investigation of any disputed response and supports FTC enforcement defense documentation.

items.label: Test retrieval pipeline against a curated adversarial query set quarterly
items.description: Maintain a library of adversarial queries (e.g., 'What is your price match guarantee?' when none exists, 'Can I return this after 90 days?' for 30-day policy items) and run regression tests against this set after every pipeline update to catch retrieval regressions before production deployment.

sections.heading: Response Generation & Output Guardrails
sections.description: LLM synthesis is where compliant source documents can still produce non-compliant outputs. Output guardrails enforce that the generated response accurately reflects retrieved evidence, includes required disclosures, and never fabricates pricing, availability, or policy terms.

items.label: Enforce source citation in every product claim response
items.description: Configure the LLM system prompt to require inline citations (e.g., '[Source: Product Spec Sheet, updated 2024-11-01]') for every factual claim about product features, pricing, or availability. Implement a post-processing parser that rejects responses missing required citations before delivery.

items.label: Implement a price output validator that cross-checks against live pricing API
items.description: Before any response containing a price figure is delivered to the customer, extract the price value and validate it against a real-time pricing API call. If the synthesized price deviates from the live price by any amount, block the response and substitute a dynamic price lookup result.

items.label: Block responses that assert return/refund terms not present in retrieved policy
items.description: Deploy a named entity extraction step that identifies return window durations, fee amounts, and eligibility conditions in the LLM output, then verifies each extracted term against the retrieved policy chunk. Any term not found in the source must trigger a response block.

items.label: Add mandatory disclosure language for promotional and sale pricing
items.description: When the RAG pipeline retrieves promotional pricing context, configure the system prompt to append required FTC disclosure language (e.g., 'Sale price valid through [date]. Original price based on [reference period].') and validate that this disclosure is present in the final output.

items.label: Implement a toxicity and bias filter on all customer-facing outputs
items.description: Run every generated response through a content safety classifier before delivery to detect and block outputs containing discriminatory language, manipulative sales tactics, or content that could constitute deceptive advertising under FTC guidelines.

items.label: Set a maximum response confidence threshold for out-of-stock and availability claims
items.description: Availability data changes faster than most ingestion pipelines can track. Configure the system to refuse to make definitive availability claims and instead always direct customers to a live inventory check endpoint, preventing the LLM from confidently asserting stock status from potentially stale context.

items.label: Implement response length and format guardrails to prevent policy truncation
items.description: Define minimum response length requirements for policy-related queries to prevent the LLM from summarizing return or refund policies in ways that omit material conditions (e.g., exclusions, restocking fees). Truncated policy summaries that omit key terms constitute misrepresentation.

items.label: Log all blocked responses with block reason codes for compliance reporting
items.description: Every response blocked by output guardrails must be logged with a structured reason code (e.g., PRICE_MISMATCH, CITATION_MISSING, FAITHFULNESS_FAIL) to enable compliance reporting, guardrail performance monitoring, and identification of systematic retrieval or generation failures.

sections.heading: Privacy, Accessibility & Operational Compliance
sections.description: Beyond content accuracy, e-commerce AI agents must comply with CCPA/GDPR data handling requirements, ADA accessibility standards for customer-facing interfaces, and operational controls that ensure the system can be audited, corrected, and shut down when necessary.

items.label: Implement PII detection and redaction before query logging
items.description: Deploy a PII scanner on all incoming customer queries before they are logged or used for retrieval. Automatically redact or tokenize names, email addresses, order numbers, and payment-adjacent data in logs to comply with CCPA and GDPR data minimization principles.

items.label: Honor CCPA/GDPR data deletion requests across RAG pipeline components
items.description: Build a deletion workflow that, upon receiving a verified consumer deletion request, removes the customer's interaction history from query logs, any personalization indexes, and any fine-tuning datasets derived from their interactions within the legally required timeframe (45 days for CCPA).

items.label: Ensure AI agent interface meets WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards
items.description: Audit the customer-facing chat or agent interface for ADA compliance: screen reader compatibility, keyboard navigation, sufficient color contrast ratios (4.5:1 minimum), and text alternatives for any non-text content. Non-compliant interfaces expose the business to ADA Title III litigation.

items.label: Implement a human escalation path for all refund and dispute queries
items.description: Configure the RAG agent to detect refund dispute and chargeback-related intents and immediately offer a human agent escalation path. Never allow the AI to make final determinations on refund eligibility—these decisions carry financial and legal consequences requiring human accountability.

items.label: Publish and maintain an AI disclosure notice on all agent interaction surfaces
items.description: Display a clear, prominent disclosure that the customer is interacting with an AI system at the start of every session, per FTC guidance on AI disclosure and emerging state-level chatbot disclosure laws. Log the timestamp of disclosure acknowledgment for each session.

items.label: Conduct quarterly bias audits on product recommendation outputs
items.description: Analyze RAG pipeline outputs across protected class proxies (e.g., geographic region, device type as a socioeconomic proxy) to detect whether the system systematically surfaces different pricing, product quality tiers, or policy terms to different customer segments, which could constitute discriminatory pricing.

items.label: Implement a kill switch and graceful degradation mode for the RAG pipeline
items.description: Build an operational kill switch that can disable the RAG pipeline within 5 minutes and fall back to a static FAQ or human agent queue. This is essential for rapid response to a detected hallucination incident, FTC inquiry, or data breach without taking the entire customer service channel offline.

notes.note: The FTC's substantiation doctrine requires that any objective product claim made by an AI agent—including performance claims, comparative claims, and testimonial-style outputs—must be supported by competent and reliable evidence at the time the claim is made. A RAG pipeline that retrieves and synthesizes unsubstantiated marketing copy is not shielded by the 'it came from a document' defense; the brand is liable for the claim as delivered. Implement pre-ingestion claim substantiation tagging and post-generation claim extraction to ensure every factual assertion in an AI response maps to a substantiated source.

Customer queries to an e-commerce RAG agent may constitute personal information under CCPA and GDPR, particularly when combined with session identifiers, purchase history used for personalization, or account context injected into the retrieval pipeline. Businesses must disclose AI-assisted data processing in their privacy policy, honor deletion and opt-out requests across all pipeline components including vector embeddings derived from customer data, and ensure data processed by third-party LLM providers is governed by a compliant data processing agreement (DPA) that restricts use for model training.

E-commerce RAG pipelines that operate within or adjacent to payment flows must ensure that the knowledge base, retrieval logs, and LLM context windows never contain cardholder data (CHD) or sensitive authentication data (SAD). Even transient exposure of card numbers in a prompt or log constitutes a PCI DSS scope violation. Implement strict data classification controls at ingestion, deploy pattern-matching blocklists for card number formats in all pipeline inputs and outputs, and ensure your LLM API provider is either out of PCI scope or covered by a signed Responsibility Matrix.

Federal courts have consistently held that e-commerce websites and their interactive features—including AI chat agents—are places of public accommodation subject to ADA Title III. A RAG-powered customer service agent that is inaccessible to users with visual, auditory, or motor disabilities exposes the business to serial ADA litigation, which has increased significantly since 2020. Ensure the agent interface meets WCAG 2.1 Level AA, that all AI-generated responses are compatible with assistive technologies, and that the human escalation path is equally accessible to users relying on screen readers or alternative input devices.

cta.heading: Automate Your E-Commerce Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your RAG pipeline for pricing accuracy, source faithfulness, and policy misrepresentation risks—so you catch compliance failures before your customers or the FTC do.
